texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Poly(dimethyl siloxane) (PDMS) thin film composite membranes originally developed for pervaporation applications were investigated for the separation of propylene from nitrogen, and the feasibility of adapting such membranes to olefin recovery from polyolefin purge gas was demonstrated. Considering that the pure gas permeation data may not be sufficient to evaluate the actual separation properties of the membrane (particularly in the case of solubility selective membranes due to strong penetrant-membrane interactions), the membrane permselectivity was extensively evaluated with propylene/nitrogen mixtures at various operating conditions (i.e., temperature, pressure, feed composition). It was shown that the membrane could be adapted to propylene/nitrogen separation, and the membrane selectivity increased with a decrease in temperature and an increase in the feed pressure. The actual permeability ratio of propylene over nitrogen in the gas mixture was lower than the permeability ratio of pure gases at the same operating conditions, but it was still sufficiently high for practical propylene/nitrogen separation. At -20 ◦ C and 500 kPa, the membrane exhibited a propylene/nitrogen permeability ratio of as high as 125 for feed mixtures containing 7-40 mol% propylene.
texts.text: Poly(dimethyl siloxane) (PDMS) thin film composite membranes originally developed for pervaporation applications were investigated for the separation of propylene from nitrogen, and the feasibility of adapting such membranes to olefin recovery from polyolefin purge gas was demonstrated. Considering that the pure gas permeation data may not be sufficient to evaluate the actual separation properties of the membrane (particularly in the case of solubility selective membranes due to strong penetrant-membrane interactions), the membrane permselectivity was extensively evaluated with propylene/nitrogen mixtures at various operating conditions (i.e., temperature, pressure, feed composition). It was shown that the membrane could be adapted to propylene/nitrogen separation, and the membrane selectivity increased with a decrease in temperature and an increase in the feed pressure. The actual permeability ratio of propylene over nitrogen in the gas mixture was lower than the permeability ratio of pure gases at the same operating conditions, but it was still sufficiently high for practical propylene/nitrogen separation. At -20 ◦ C and 500 kPa, the membrane exhibited a propylene/nitrogen permeability ratio of as high as 125 for feed mixtures containing 7-40 mol% propylene.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyolefins, primarily polyethylene and polypropylene, comprise the largest share of the polymer market. Polyolefin manufacturing is an important operation in the petrochemical industry. In typical polymerization processes, the raw polyolefin product from a polymerization reactor contains a considerable amount of un-reacted olefin monomer. To remove the monomer, the raw polymer resin is purged with nitrogen to strip the monomer out. Due to the high cost of the monomers, the monomer in the off gas stream represents a significant source of profit if recovered and reused.
texts.text: Polyolefins, primarily polyethylene and polypropylene, comprise the largest share of the polymer market. Polyolefin manufacturing is an important operation in the petrochemical industry. In typical polymerization processes, the raw polyolefin product from a polymerization reactor contains a considerable amount of un-reacted olefin monomer. To remove the monomer, the raw polymer resin is purged with nitrogen to strip the monomer out. Due to the high cost of the monomers, the monomer in the off gas stream represents a significant source of profit if recovered and reused.

It is a strong contender and complement to such conventional processes as carbon adsorption and condensation, and the payback time of a membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Generally speaking, sorption and diffusion are the governing factors for gas permeation through a dense polymeric membrane. While the diffusion step favors small molecules, the sorption step favors large and condensable molecules. To permeate olefin molecules preferentially, rubbery membranes are preferred in terms of permeability and selectivity, taking advantage of the favorable solubility properties of rubbery polymers for olefins over nitrogen. Currently, poly(dimethyl siloxane) (PDMS) is the primary polymer used for olefin separations. In the mid-1950s, PDMS had been found to have a much higher permeability to gases than almost all other synthetic polymers known at that time [2], and even nowadays PDMS is still considered to be one of the most permeable polymers. The high permeability of PDMS is attributed to its flexible siloxane linkages. The flexible polymer chain with long range segmental motions that are active even at

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In spite of the recent industrial success of PDMS-based membranes for olefin recovery from polyolefin resin purge gases, not much information is available in the literature regarding the permeability and selectivity of the membrane to olefins at various operating conditions (e.g., temperature, pressure, and feed composition). In addition, there are only a few commercial suppliers worldwide for olefin recovery membranes. GFT, a pioneer company in the industrial development and commercialization of pervaporation membranes for liquid separations, has developed a PDMS thin film composite membrane for organic removal from aqueous solutions. Many studies have been done on the application of the organophilic membrane for removing various organic compounds from water (see for example [4-8]). However, the commercial success of the membrane for pervaporation separation of organic compounds from aqueous mixtures has been rather limited primarily due to inadequate market demand and economic drive at the present time. Nevertheless, this membrane could be well suited for olefin recovery, considering its desired structure of a thin skin layer of PDMS supported on a microporous substrate. The objectives of this work are to investigate the performance of this membrane for the separation of propylene from nitrogen and to demonstrate the feasibility of adapting the thin film composite PDMS pervaporation membranes to olefin recovery. This information will not only help expand the applications of the membrane to include gas separations but also enhance the availability of commercial olefin recovery membranes in the market.

Many studies have been done on the application of the organophilic membrane for removing various organic compounds from water (see for example [4-8]). However, the commercial success of the membrane for pervaporation separation of organic compounds from aqueous mixtures has been rather limited primarily due to inadequate market demand and economic drive at the present time. Nevertheless, this membrane could be well suited for olefin recovery, considering its desired structure of a thin skin layer of PDMS supported on a microporous substrate. The objectives of this work are to investigate the performance of this membrane for the separation of propylene from nitrogen and to demonstrate the feasibility of adapting the thin film composite PDMS pervaporation membranes to olefin recovery. This information will not only help expand the applications of the membrane to include gas separations but also enhance the availability of commercial olefin recovery membranes in the market.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, the membrane permselectivity was evaluated, and the effects of operating temperature and pressure on the membrane performance were investigated. Note that unlike many studies reported in the literature that often deal with only pure gas permeation for the sake of simplicity in measurements, the membrane permselectivity for the permeation of propylene/nitrogen mixtures was also investigated extensively, because the pure gas permeation data may not be sufficient to evaluate the actual separation properties of such membranes, particularly in the case of condensable gases where there often exist strong permeant-membrane interactions.
texts.text: In this work, the membrane permselectivity was evaluated, and the effects of operating temperature and pressure on the membrane performance were investigated. Note that unlike many studies reported in the literature that often deal with only pure gas permeation for the sake of simplicity in measurements, the membrane permselectivity for the permeation of propylene/nitrogen mixtures was also investigated extensively, because the pure gas permeation data may not be sufficient to evaluate the actual separation properties of such membranes, particularly in the case of condensable gases where there often exist strong permeant-membrane interactions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Experimental
texts.text: 2. Experimental

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PDMS membrane (Pervap 1070) was manufactured by GFT (now Sulzer Chemtech). It is comprised of a thin selective skin layer of PDMS filled with a certain amount of hydrophobic ZSM-5 zeolite particles and a microporous asymmetric polyacrylonitrile substrate, which is backed on a nonwoven polyester fabric for increased mechanical strength. To determine the thickness of the selective PDMS layer, a small piece of the membrane wasdelaminatedandtheskinlayerwasmeasuredtobeca.38 𝛍 m thick by a Mitutoyo micrometer.
texts.text: The PDMS membrane (Pervap 1070) was manufactured by GFT (now Sulzer Chemtech). It is comprised of a thin selective skin layer of PDMS filled with a certain amount of hydrophobic ZSM-5 zeolite particles and a microporous asymmetric polyacrylonitrile substrate, which is backed on a nonwoven polyester fabric for increased mechanical strength. To determine the thickness of the selective PDMS layer, a small piece of the membrane wasdelaminatedandtheskinlayerwasmeasuredtobeca.38 𝛍 m thick by a Mitutoyo micrometer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The experimental setup is schematically shown in Fig. 1. The feed gas at a given composition was obtained using a dynamic gas blending system comprised of two Matheson gas flow controllers, and the actual composition of the gas mixture was determined using a Varian (CP-3800) gas chromatograph equipped with a thermal conductivity detector. The membrane was cut into circular coupons and fitted in the permeation cell, which was placed in an Ultra-Kroymat thermal bath that was capable of controlling the temperature with a precision of ± 0.1 ◦ C. The permeation cell consisted of two detachable stainless steel parts. Asintered porous stainless steel plate was imbedded in one part of the cell to support the membrane. Double O-rings were used to provide a seal between the membrane and the cell. The effective area of the membrane for permeation was 9.62 cm 2 . The feed gas was pre-cooled or -heated to the operating temperature in a coil of copper tubing and then admitted to the permeation cell. The volumetric flow rate of the permeate was measured by a bubble flow meter, and the residue flow was kept at a much higher flow rate so as to maintain a low stage cut (<0.05). At such a low stage cut, the variation in the feed composition in the membrane cell was negligibly small, which allows for determination of the membrane permselectivity at a given feed concentration. This study dealt with steady state permeation, and the steady state was assumed to be reached when the flow rate and composition of the permeate stream became constant.
texts.text: The experimental setup is schematically shown in Fig. 1. The feed gas at a given composition was obtained using a dynamic gas blending system comprised of two Matheson gas flow controllers, and the actual composition of the gas mixture was determined using a Varian (CP-3800) gas chromatograph equipped with a thermal conductivity detector. The membrane was cut into circular coupons and fitted in the permeation cell, which was placed in an Ultra-Kroymat thermal bath that was capable of controlling the temperature with a precision of ± 0.1 ◦ C. The permeation cell consisted of two detachable stainless steel parts. Asintered porous stainless steel plate was imbedded in one part of the cell to support the membrane. Double O-rings were used to provide a seal between the membrane and the cell. The effective area of the membrane for permeation was 9.62 cm 2 . The feed gas was pre-cooled or -heated to the operating temperature in a coil of copper tubing and then admitted to the permeation cell. The volumetric flow rate of the permeate was measured by a bubble flow meter, and the residue flow was kept at a much higher flow rate so as to maintain a low stage cut (<0.05). At such a low stage cut, the variation in the feed composition in the membrane cell was negligibly small, which allows for determination of the membrane permselectivity at a given feed concentration. This study dealt with steady state permeation, and the steady state was assumed to be reached when the flow rate and composition of the permeate stream became constant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the membrane used was a thin film composite membrane that was originally designed for liquid separation by pervaporation where the permeate side was evacuated, it was decided to determine whether the substrate imposed a significant resistance to mass transfer through the membrane for gas permeation.

texts.orig: The same experimental setup and procedure were also used for pure gas permeation by simply switching off one of the mass flow controllers in the gas blending system, except that it was no longer necessary to maintain a high residue flow rate in pure gas permeation. The experimental error for pure gas permeance measurements was estimated to be within 5%, and the experimental error in permeance measurements of individual components, which involved composition analysis, was about 8%.

The experimental error for pure gas permeance measurements was estimated to be within 5%, and the experimental error in permeance measurements of individual components, which involved composition analysis, was about 8%.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Pure gas permeation
texts.text: 3.1. Pure gas permeation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane was first tested for permselectivity based on pure gas permeation to determine the possibility of adapting the pervaporation membrane to propylene/nitrogen separation. Figs. 2 and 3 show the permeability coefficients of pure nitrogen and propylene through the membrane at different temperatures and pressures. It can be seen that the permeability of nitrogen is essentially independent of pressure at relatively low temper-
texts.text: The membrane was first tested for permselectivity based on pure gas permeation to determine the possibility of adapting the pervaporation membrane to propylene/nitrogen separation. Figs. 2 and 3 show the permeability coefficients of pure nitrogen and propylene through the membrane at different temperatures and pressures. It can be seen that the permeability of nitrogen is essentially independent of pressure at relatively low temper-

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2. Permeability coefficient of nitrogen at different feed pressures and temperatures.
texts.text: Fig. 2. Permeability coefficient of nitrogen at different feed pressures and temperatures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: atures, and it begins to increase slightly with pressure at higher temperatures. In spite of the fact that the membrane contains hydrophobic zeolite fillers, the membrane permeability does not appear to be considerably compromised as it is very close to the permeability of pure PDMS membranes reported; the nitrogen permeability coefficients in PDMS measured by various researchers are summarized in Table 1. On the other hand, the permeability of propylene increases significantly with an increase in pressure, especially at low temperatures. Compared to nitrogen, the permeability of propylene is strongly affected by temperature. This behavior is typical of rubbery polymer membranes and the observations are consistent with the physical reasoning that propylene sorbed in the membrane will swell the polymer matrix resulting in increased solubility and diffusivity. An increase in solubility with an increase in pressure and a decrease in solubility with increasing temperature are normally expected. It appears that the solubility plays a major role in the permeation of propylene. Liu et al. [12] reported similar results for propylene permeation through rubbery poly(ether block amide) (PEBA 2533) membranes.

On the other hand, the permeability of propylene increases significantly with an increase in pressure, especially at low temperatures. Compared to nitrogen, the permeability of propylene is strongly affected by temperature. This behavior is typical of rubbery polymer membranes and the observations are consistent with the physical reasoning that propylene sorbed in the membrane will swell the polymer matrix resulting in increased solubility and diffusivity. An increase in solubility with an increase in pressure and a decrease in solubility with increasing temperature are normally expected. It appears that the solubility plays a major role in the permeation of propylene. Liu et al. [12] reported similar results for propylene permeation through rubbery poly(ether block amide) (PEBA 2533) membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It would be of interest to study how the ZSM-5 fillers in the membrane influence the membrane selectivity for propylene/nitrogen separation. Unfortunately, the information on the PDMS polymer (e.g., pre-polymer, cross-linker, and catalyst), the zeolite fillers (e.g., composition, crystal size, pore structure) and the orientation and particle size of the zeolite fillers in the membrane is unavailable due to proprietary considerations, and it is thus difficult to evaluate the sorption/adsorption and diffusion behavior of the gases in the filler-free PDMS and the zeolite individually. Nevertheless, a study with a PDMS membrane prepared from RTV 655 silicone rubber (which will be reported separately) showed that both propylene and nitrogen permeation followed similar trends as in the composite membrane studied here in terms of pressure, temperature and composition dependencies of the membrane permeability. This indicates that the zeolite fillers in the composite membrane do not have sufficient impact to change the membrane behavior significantly. In pervaporation of water-organic mixtures, the hydrophobicity of the zeolite fillers in the membrane will restrict the permeation of water, thereby improving the membrane selectivity to organic permeation. However, in gas separation, the influence of the fillers becomes more complicated as both diffusion rate and adsorption coverage in the fillers affect the selectivity. While the diffusion favors nitrogen permeation because of its smaller kinetic diameter (0.364 nm) than propylene (0.450 nm) [13], the adsorption affinity of a hydrocarbon to the zeolite tends to be stronger than nitrogen [14], which favors preferential sorption of propylene over nitrogen. On the other hand, when a condensable gas is strongly adsorbed, pore blocking of the zeolite may occur, and this will also affect the desorption rate. A detailed study of the gas-filler interactions, which is beyond the scope of the current work, will be needed to gain an insight into the role of the zeolite fillers in the membrane.
texts.text: It would be of interest to study how the ZSM-5 fillers in the membrane influence the membrane selectivity for propylene/nitrogen separation. Unfortunately, the information on the PDMS polymer (e.g., pre-polymer, cross-linker, and catalyst), the zeolite fillers (e.g., composition, crystal size, pore structure) and the orientation and particle size of the zeolite fillers in the membrane is unavailable due to proprietary considerations, and it is thus difficult to evaluate the sorption/adsorption and diffusion behavior of the gases in the filler-free PDMS and the zeolite individually. Nevertheless, a study with a PDMS membrane prepared from RTV 655 silicone rubber (which will be reported separately) showed that both propylene and nitrogen permeation followed similar trends as in the composite membrane studied here in terms of pressure, temperature and composition dependencies of the membrane permeability. This indicates that the zeolite fillers in the composite membrane do not have sufficient impact to change the membrane behavior significantly. In pervaporation of water-organic mixtures, the hydrophobicity of the zeolite fillers in the membrane will restrict the permeation of water, thereby improving the membrane selectivity to organic permeation. However, in gas separation, the influence of the fillers becomes more complicated as both diffusion rate and adsorption coverage in the fillers affect the selectivity. While the diffusion favors nitrogen permeation because of its smaller kinetic diameter (0.364 nm) than propylene (0.450 nm) [13], the adsorption affinity of a hydrocarbon to the zeolite tends to be stronger than nitrogen [14], which favors preferential sorption of propylene over nitrogen. On the other hand, when a condensable gas is strongly adsorbed, pore blocking of the zeolite may occur, and this will also affect the desorption rate. A detailed study of the gas-filler interactions, which is beyond the scope of the current work, will be needed to gain an insight into the role of the zeolite fillers in the membrane.

This appears to be the case for propylene. Similar behavior has been confirmed for propylene permeation through rubbery PEBA membranes [15].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In actual gas separations, the feed gas involved is a mixture, andthepuregaspermeationdataareusuallynotsufficient to estimate the actual separation performance of the membrane, particularly in cases where there are specific interactions between the gas components and the polymer matrix. As shown above, the propylene permeability is significantly affected by pressure, and the strong pressure dependence is an indication of strong interaction between propylene and the membrane material. Therefore, the actual membrane permselectivity for gas mixture permeation was also investigated.

texts.orig: with an increase in the feed pressure. At a given feed pressure, the higher the feed propylene concentration is, the higher the permeation flux and the permeate propylene concentration are. Thepartial fluxes of propylene and nitrogen are plotted as a function of their partial pressure differences across the membrane, as shown in Figs. 9 and 10, respectively. It is interesting to notice from the data in Fig. 9 that the partial fluxes of propylene are essentially the same as the permeation flux of pure propylene at the same transmembrane driving force for permeation (i.e. the partial pressure difference). Consequently, the permeability coefficient of propylene, which is equal to the partial flux normalized by the partial pressure difference across the membrane, is quite close to the pure propylene permeability, as shown in Fig. 11. This means that the presence of nitrogen does not appear to affect considerably the permeability of the membrane to propylene. Similar results have been reported for the separation of organic vapors from nitrogen using PDMS membranes. Singh et al. [9] observed that the permeability coefficient of acetone through a PDMS membrane was independent of nitrogen concentration in the mixture and was equal to the pure acetone permeability coefficient, and Dixon-Garrett et al. [17] found the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: permeability of ethylbenzene in an ethylbenzene/nitrogen mixture was similar to pure ethylbenzene permeability. This may be explained from the standpoint of penetrant-membrane interactions. As discussed earlier, compared to propylene, which is a condensable gas that interacts strongly with the rubbery polymers as reflected by its high solubility and strong pressure dependence of permeability, nitrogen, a permanent gas, appears to have little interaction with the membrane. This also explains why the permeability of nitrogen in the mixture is significantly affected by the presence of propylene (see Fig. 10). The impact of gas composition on the permeability coefficient of nitrogen is plotted in Fig. 12. It is shown that while the permeability of pure nitrogen is essentially independent of pressure, the permeability coefficient of nitrogen in propylene/nitrogen mixtures increases with an increase in the propylene content in the feed. An increase in the partial pressure of propylene in the feed due to increased feed pressure or increased feed propylene concentration will increase the amount of propylene dissolved in the membrane, rendering the membrane more swollen by the pene-

The impact of gas composition on the permeability coefficient of nitrogen is plotted in Fig. 12. It is shown that while the permeability of pure nitrogen is essentially independent of pressure, the permeability coefficient of nitrogen in propylene/nitrogen mixtures increases with an increase in the propylene content in the feed. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane selectivity measured in terms of propylene/nitrogen permeance ratio is shown in Fig. 13. The permeance ratios of pure gases are included for comparison. It is shown that the membrane selectivity as measured by the permeance ratio tends to increase with an increase in the feed pressure and feed propylene concentration. However, for a given feed pressure, the permeance ratio of propylene over nitrogen in their mixtures is much lower than the pure gas permeance ratio. This is due to two factors based on the above observations. One is that the presence of olefin in the gas mixture increases the permeance of nitrogen, and the other is that the permeability of olefin in a mixture is lower than pure olefin permeability at the same feed pressure because of the lower partial olefin pressures in the mixtures. Although pure gas permeance ratio is often conveniently used to characterize membrane selectivity, the data in Fig. 13 indicate that it cannot be taken for granted when evaluating the actual selectivity of the membrane for separation of gas mixtures. It should be pointed out that the gas mixture permeation has also been carried out at higher temperatures ( -10, 0, 10, and 25 ◦ C), and in general the membrane performance followed similar trends except that the difference between pure gas permeance ratio and the permeance ratio of the propylene and nitrogen in the gas mixtures became smaller as the temperature increased.
texts.text: The membrane selectivity measured in terms of propylene/nitrogen permeance ratio is shown in Fig. 13. The permeance ratios of pure gases are included for comparison. It is shown that the membrane selectivity as measured by the permeance ratio tends to increase with an increase in the feed pressure and feed propylene concentration. However, for a given feed pressure, the permeance ratio of propylene over nitrogen in their mixtures is much lower than the pure gas permeance ratio. This is due to two factors based on the above observations. One is that the presence of olefin in the gas mixture increases the permeance of nitrogen, and the other is that the permeability of olefin in a mixture is lower than pure olefin permeability at the same feed pressure because of the lower partial olefin pressures in the mixtures. Although pure gas permeance ratio is often conveniently used to characterize membrane selectivity, the data in Fig. 13 indicate that it cannot be taken for granted when evaluating the actual selectivity of the membrane for separation of gas mixtures. It should be pointed out that the gas mixture permeation has also been carried out at higher temperatures ( -10, 0, 10, and 25 ◦ C), and in general the membrane performance followed similar trends except that the difference between pure gas permeance ratio and the permeance ratio of the propylene and nitrogen in the gas mixtures became smaller as the temperature increased.

In either case, the separation factor is always smaller than the permeance ratio, as illustrated in Fig. 15 where α / β is plotted versus P l / P h for different permeance ratios. Only when the permeate pressure P l is negligibly small as compared to the feed pressure P h will the numerical value of the separation factor approach the permeance ratio. This is obviously not the case under normal operating conditions for gas separations.

texts.orig: As the data in Figs. 4 and 5 imply, the operating temperature has a significant effect on the membrane permselectivity. The effects of temperature on the permeability of propylene and nitrogen in their mixtures were determined at different feed pressures and compositions. It was found that in spite of the coupling effect in mixture permeation observed earlier, the temperature dependence of permeability for both components follows the Arrhenius-type of relation. Fig. 16 shows the typical results for a feed propylene/nitrogen mixture containing 10 mol% propylene. Consistent with pure gas permeation, the permeability coefficient of nitrogen increases with temperature, and is not largely affected by the feed pressure. On the other hand, an increase in

It was found that in spite of the coupling effect in mixture permeation observed earlier, the temperature dependence of permeability for both components follows the Arrhenius-type of relation. Fig. 16 shows the typical results for a feed propylene/nitrogen mixture containing 10 mol% propylene. Consistent with pure gas permeation, the permeability coefficient of nitrogen increases with temperature, and is not largely affected by the feed pressure. On the other hand, an increase in

texts.orig: Fig. 16. Temperature dependence of propylene and nitrogen permeabilities for gas mixture permeation at different feed pressures. Feed propylene concentration 10 mol%.
texts.text: Fig. 16. Temperature dependence of propylene and nitrogen permeabilities for gas mixture permeation at different feed pressures. Feed propylene concentration 10 mol%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: temperature will cause the propylene permeability to decrease or slightly increase, depending on the feed pressure. Temperature has two opposite effects on the permeability. While an increase in temperature tends to increase the thermal motion of the polymer segments thereby increasing the diffusivity, the solubility generally decreases with temperature, rendering the membraneless swollen, which in turn affects the diffusivity negatively due to reduced free volume and polymer chain flexibility. Similar behavior has been observed with rubbery poly(ether block amide) membranes [12]. At a high feed pressure (and thus a high propylene partial pressure), the membrane is considerably swollen by the propylene dissolved, and the temperature is likely to have a more significant effect on the solubility than in a situation where the permeant uptake in the membrane is relatively low. Consequently, when the negative effects of reduced solubility and diffusivity caused by temperature increase cannot be overcome by the positive effect of diffusivity increase due to enhanced thermal motion of polymer chains, a decrease in propylene permeability with an increase in temperature will result. In addition, the data in the figure also show that the propylene permeability is more sensitive to pressure at lower temperatures, which is consistent with the results obtained from pure propylene permeation. Note that when the feed propylene concentration is sufficiently high, the nitrogen permeability begins to be influenced by the feed pressure more notably, as illustrated in Fig. 17 where the permeabilities of propylene and nitrogen at a feed propylene concentration of 40 mol% are presented.
texts.text: temperature will cause the propylene permeability to decrease or slightly increase, depending on the feed pressure. Temperature has two opposite effects on the permeability. While an increase in temperature tends to increase the thermal motion of the polymer segments thereby increasing the diffusivity, the solubility generally decreases with temperature, rendering the membraneless swollen, which in turn affects the diffusivity negatively due to reduced free volume and polymer chain flexibility. Similar behavior has been observed with rubbery poly(ether block amide) membranes [12]. At a high feed pressure (and thus a high propylene partial pressure), the membrane is considerably swollen by the propylene dissolved, and the temperature is likely to have a more significant effect on the solubility than in a situation where the permeant uptake in the membrane is relatively low. Consequently, when the negative effects of reduced solubility and diffusivity caused by temperature increase cannot be overcome by the positive effect of diffusivity increase due to enhanced thermal motion of polymer chains, a decrease in propylene permeability with an increase in temperature will result. In addition, the data in the figure also show that the propylene permeability is more sensitive to pressure at lower temperatures, which is consistent with the results obtained from pure propylene permeation. Note that when the feed propylene concentration is sufficiently high, the nitrogen permeability begins to be influenced by the feed pressure more notably, as illustrated in Fig. 17 where the permeabilities of propylene and nitrogen at a feed propylene concentration of 40 mol% are presented.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permselectivity of the PDMS composite membrane for propylene/nitrogen separation was investigated, and the following conclusions can be drawn:
texts.text: The permselectivity of the PDMS composite membrane for propylene/nitrogen separation was investigated, and the following conclusions can be drawn:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) Although originally developed for pervaporation applications, the PDMS composite membrane can be adapted to propylene separation from nitrogen that is relevant to propylene recovery from polypropylene degassing off gas.
texts.text: Although originally developed for pervaporation applications, the PDMS composite membrane can be adapted to propylene separation from nitrogen that is relevant to propylene recovery from polypropylene degassing off gas.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (2) The propylene/nitrogen selectivity can be enhanced by lowering the operating temperature and increasing the feed gas pressure. At a given operating temperature and pressure, the membrane selectivity tends to be higher as feed propylene concentration increases.
texts.text: The propylene/nitrogen selectivity can be enhanced by lowering the operating temperature and increasing the feed gas pressure. At a given operating temperature and pressure, the membrane selectivity tends to be higher as feed propylene concentration increases.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (3) The actual permeability ratio of propylene over nitrogen in the gas mixtures is much lower than the permeability ratio of the pure gases at the same operating conditions, but it is still sufficiently high to separate propylene from nitrogen efficiently. At -20 ◦ C and 500 kPa, a propylene/nitrogen permeability ratio of as high as 125 was obtained for feed mixtures containing 7-40 mol% propylene.
texts.text: The actual permeability ratio of propylene over nitrogen in the gas mixtures is much lower than the permeability ratio of the pure gases at the same operating conditions, but it is still sufficiently high to separate propylene from nitrogen efficiently. At -20 ◦ C and 500 kPa, a propylene/nitrogen permeability ratio of as high as 125 was obtained for feed mixtures containing 7-40 mol% propylene.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (4) For gas mixture separation, the permeability of propylene is not largely affected by the presence of nitrogen, but nitrogen permeability is significantly enhanced by the presence of propylene.
texts.text: For gas mixture separation, the permeability of propylene is not largely affected by the presence of nitrogen, but nitrogen permeability is significantly enhanced by the presence of propylene.
